metadata.summary: LlamaIndex chat engine modes (condense_plus_context, context, react), ChatMemoryBuffer token management, and streaming responses for document-grounded multi-turn conversations.

content.title: LlamaIndex Chat Engine and Streaming
content.summary: LlamaIndex chat engines wrap a VectorStoreIndex with conversation memory and follow-up question handling. Choose chat mode based on follow-up complexity: condense_plus_context for standard Q&A with history, react for tool-using agents. Bound memory with ChatMemoryBuffer(token_limit=3900) to prevent context overflow. Use streaming for responsive UX in production.

why.p: Standard query engines are stateless: each query is independent. Chat engines maintain conversation history and optionally rewrite follow-up questions into standalone queries before retrieval. Without condensation, follow-ups like "tell me more" retrieve nothing — the query engine sees no context. condense_plus_context mode solves this by first condensing history+follow-up into a standalone query, then augmenting it with retrieved context.

Memory bounding is non-negotiable in long conversations. Unbounded history grows the prompt token count until the LLM context window overflows. ChatMemoryBuffer enforces a token limit, truncating old messages to stay within budget.

list.item: Document Q&A with follow-up questions — users refer to previous answers ("what did you say about X?").
Customer support bots grounded in a knowledge base — context mode injects relevant documents into each turn.
Long-running research sessions where conversation history helps the agent track what has been covered.
Production UX requiring streaming — users see words appear as the model generates, not a long wait.
Single-turn Q&A with no follow-up — a query engine is simpler and cheaper (no history overhead).
High-throughput batch querying — chat engines maintain per-session state; use query engines with async batching instead.
The primary task is tool orchestration with complex state — LangGraph or a custom ReAct loop has better control primitives.

section.name: chat-modes
section.p: LlamaIndex provides five chat modes. The three most useful in production: condense_plus_context, context, and react.

rule.statement: Default to condense_plus_context for document Q&A with conversation history. Use context when history condensation is not needed (each turn is independent). Use react only when the chat engine needs to invoke tools dynamically during the conversation.
rule.rationale: condense_question alone rewrites the query but does not inject retrieved context — the LLM answers from memory. context alone injects context but does not handle follow-ups. condense_plus_context combines both, making it the safe default for document-grounded chat.

example.title: condense_plus_context with bounded memory
example.code:
from llama_index.core.memory import ChatMemoryBuffer
memory = ChatMemoryBuffer.from_defaults(token_limit=3900)
chat_engine = index.as_chat_engine( chat_mode="condense_plus_context", memory=memory, system_prompt=( "You are a helpful assistant answering questions " "about documents. Use only the provided context." ), verbose=True, )
response = chat_engine.chat("What is this document about?") print(response)
response = chat_engine.chat("Can you give me an example from it?") print(response)
example.note: token_limit=3900 leaves room for retrieved context and generation within an 8k context window.

example.title: Unbounded memory in a long session
example.code: # Do NOT omit token_limit for long sessions chat_engine = index.as_chat_engine( chat_mode="condense_plus_context", )
example.note: History grows without bound. After ~20 turns on a verbose topic, the prompt overflows the context window and the LLM errors or silently truncates early turns.

section.name: chat-mode-reference
section.p: Mode selection reference for common scenarios.

list.item: condense_question: rewrites follow-up into standalone query using history; does NOT inject retrieved context — answer comes from LLM memory only. Use for conversational rephrasing without RAG.
context: retrieves documents for every turn and injects them; does NOT condense follow-ups — "tell me more" retrieves nothing relevant. Use when each turn is semantically complete.
condense_plus_context: condenses follow-up into standalone query, then retrieves and injects context. Best for document-grounded multi-turn chat.
simple: no context augmentation, no condensation. Equivalent to a plain LLM chat. Use only for non-RAG turns.
react: ReAct agent with tools exposed as chat turns. Use when the chat engine needs to call tools (search, calculator, code executor) during conversation.

section.name: streaming
section.p: Streaming sends tokens to the client as they are generated, avoiding a long wait. LlamaIndex streaming works for both query engines and chat engines.

rule.statement: Enable streaming=True on any user-facing query or chat engine. Consume response_gen in a loop; do not buffer the full response before display. For async applications, use astream_chat() and async for.
rule.rationale: LLM generation latency is dominated by the first token wait plus token-by-token delivery. Streaming eliminates the perceived latency: users see the first word in ~1 second instead of waiting for the full paragraph. Buffering the full stream before display negates this benefit.

example.title: Streaming query engine response
example.code:
query_engine = index.as_query_engine(streaming=True) streaming_response = query_engine.query("What is the main topic?")
for text in streaming_response.response_gen: print(text, end="", flush=True) print() # newline after stream completes

example.title: Async streaming chat engine
example.code:
from llama_index.core.memory import ChatMemoryBuffer
memory = ChatMemoryBuffer.from_defaults(token_limit=3900) chat_engine = index.as_chat_engine( chat_mode="condense_plus_context", memory=memory, )
async def stream_chat(query: str) -> None: response = await chat_engine.astream_chat(query) async for token in response.async_response_gen(): print(token, end="", flush=True) print()
example.note: astream_chat() does not block the event loop — required when the chat engine runs inside an async agent or web server.

section.name: agent-integration-patterns
section.p: When building an agent that uses a chat engine, the agentic workflow selects the retrieval strategy based on query characteristics and routes through the chat engine for context-augmented generation.

Recommended subagent pattern: a planner subagent decides whether the query requires multi-document synthesis (SubQuestionQueryEngine) or single-document Q&A (chat engine). The chat engine handles the latter, maintaining session state across the agent's conversation turns.
section.prompt: You are a multi-document research agent. Load indexes from paths {index_paths}. For query "{query}": 1. If query compares multiple sources -> use SubQuestionQueryEngine across all indexes 2. If query is factual about one source -> use VectorRetriever on the relevant index via chat engine 3. Always rerank top-20 to top-5 before synthesis Return JSON: {"answer": str, "strategy_used": str, "sources": [{"index": str, "text": str}]}

checklist.check: Set token_limit on ChatMemoryBuffer before deploying any chat engine to production.
Default to condense_plus_context mode for document-grounded multi-turn Q&A.
Enable streaming=True on all user-facing query and chat engines.
Use astream_chat() (not stream_chat()) inside async agent loops to avoid blocking the event loop.
Reset chat engine history between unrelated sessions to prevent cross-session context leakage.
